title: Slow-cooker beef stew with dumplings
subtitle: Eight hours low and slow, dumplings added for the last hour
excerpt: Beef chuck braised with root vegetables and stock until the meat yields completely, topped with self-raising flour dumplings for the final hour. The slow cooker does the work. Build it in the morning, add the dumplings an hour before serving.
sourceNotes: Beef stew with dumplings is a British comfort-food standard appearing in every post-war household cookbook. The slow cooker version became widespread after the appliance gained popularity in the 1970s and 1980s. The dumpling ratio (self-raising flour, half-fat fat, pinch of salt, enough water to bind) has not changed: it produces a dense, soft dumpling that absorbs gravy from below and steams from above.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the stew without the dumplings. Make fresh dumplings when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The stew improves overnight. Refrigerate, skim the fat, reheat gently and make fresh dumplings.

content.text: Browning the beef before it goes into the slow cooker is worth doing. It takes 10 minutes and adds a depth to the finished stew that the slow cooker cannot provide on its own. If time is short, skip it — the result is still good, just paler and milder.

Do not add the dumplings until the last hour. They need the lid on and the heat high enough to steam through — add them too early and they turn gluey. The lid must stay on for the full hour once the dumplings are in.
Brown the beef and build the pot

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide frying pan over high heat. Season the {{beef-chuck}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Brown in batches — do not crowd the pan. Each batch needs 2 to 3 minutes per side until deeply coloured. Transfer each batch to the slow cooker. Add the {{onion}} to the same pan and fry for 3 minutes, scraping up any browned bits. Add the {{garlic}} and cook for 1 minute. Stir in the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{stock-beef}} and {{worcestershire-sauce}}, bring to a simmer, then pour over the beef in the slow cooker. Add the {{carrot}}, {{potato}}, {{thyme-fresh}}, and {{bay-leaves}}. Cook on low for 8 to 9 hours, until the beef is tender.

About 1 hour before serving, make the dumplings. Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, mixing to a soft, slightly sticky dough — about 4 to 5 tablespoons total. Shape into 12 balls. Remove the lid from the slow cooker, switch to high, and place the dumplings on top of the stew. Replace the lid and cook on high for 1 hour without lifting the lid. The dumplings should have puffed and be cooked through. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Dumplings dense and doughy in the middle
items.cause: Lid lifted during the dumpling cooking hour, or pot not hot enough
items.fix: Switch to high before adding the dumplings and do not lift the lid at all for the full hour. The steam cooks the dumplings from above — any lost steam means undercooked centres

items.cause: Lid not sealing well, releasing too much steam
items.fix: Mix 2 tablespoons of {{cornflour}} with 2 tablespoons of cold water and stir into the stew in the last 30 minutes before the dumplings go in. Alternatively, remove the lid for the last 30 minutes to reduce

items.cause: Slow cooker running colder than expected, or cut used was too lean
items.fix: Chuck is the right cut — it has enough collagen to break down over 8 hours. Add another hour on low if it is still resistant

content.text: Beef stew with dumplings is a cold-weather British dinner, made on days when something needs to be ready without effort when the household comes home. The slow cooker version is one of the strongest arguments for the appliance: set it before leaving for work, come back to a finished dinner. Leftovers improve overnight as the stew thickens and the flavours deepen.
